does: This paper formally defines calibration for probabilistic label ranking—the task of predicting probability distributions over orderings of multiple labels. The authors introduce a hierarchy of calibration notions covering full rankings, sub-rankings (predictions over subsets of labels), and top-k rankings, and prove logical relationships between them, showing that full-rank calibration implies sub-ranking and top-k calibration but not vice versa. Through empirical evaluation, they demonstrate that popular label ranking models exhibit poor calibration across different metrics, with substantial gaps between sub-ranking and top-k calibration scores, and apply their framework to RLHF reward models where calibration correlates with but extends beyond top-1 accuracy.
connects: This work extends the well-established notion of calibration from binary and multiclass classification to the structured prediction setting of label ranking. Calibration has long been recognized as crucial for reliable probabilistic predictions, with extensive prior work on detecting and correcting miscalibration in classifiers. Label ranking itself builds on preference learning and ranking literature, which have addressed point estimates and scoring functions but lacked formal treatment of distributional calibration. The paper bridges these communities by recognizing that treating rankings as flat classes loses the inherent structure—pairwise preferences and relative orderings—that classical calibration measures fail to capture appropriately.
enables: This framework opens investigation into calibration-improving methods specifically designed for ranking tasks, moving beyond naive application of classification calibration techniques. The findings suggest developing post-hoc calibration methods and training procedures that target different levels of ranking calibration simultaneously. For RLHF and reward modeling, the framework enables better evaluation of language model training signals, potentially improving alignment by selecting reward models based on probabilistic quality beyond accuracy. The hierarchy of calibration notions also invites future work on understanding when full-rank versus partial calibration matters for downstream applications, and on developing efficient algorithms for computing these diverse calibration metrics at scale.
